module: Module 1. Ethics, Knowledge, Learning, and Intelligence
proposed module classification / title: Module 1. AI-Mediated World: Purposes, Values, Knowledge, and Intelligence
lecture: Why Is AI a Public and Ethical Issue?
subtitle: Frame AI as a technical project shaped by public values, institutions, and ethical choices.

rationale: Start with concrete systems students already encounter so the course feels broad and grounded before moving into ethics, knowledge, and intelligence.
proposed module title: Module 1. AI-Mediated World: Purposes, Values, and Questions
proposed topic: Where Is AI Mediating the World Around Us?
proposed subtitle/description: Map AI systems students already encounter across everyday life, institutions, infrastructure, and culture. Introduce the five guiding questions as the analytical frame students will use all semester.
proposed rationale for the change: Keep as opening session. Also use it to introduce the 5 guiding questions explicitly so students have the analytical lens from day one.

module: Module 1. Ethics, Knowledge, Learning, and Intelligence
proposed module classification / title: Module 1. AI-Mediated World: Purposes, Values, Knowledge, and Intelligence
lecture: What Is AI Supposed to Help Us Do?
subtitle: Examine how ideas about safety, care, efficiency, creativity, and improvement shape what AI is imagined to be for.

revised subtitle: Frame AI as a technical project that redistributes visibility, authority, risk, and responsibility in public life.
rationale: Move the public/ethical framing after students have mapped the AI-mediated world; this makes the stakes feel less abstract.
proposed module title: Module 1. AI-Mediated World: Purposes, Values, and Questions
proposed topic: Why Is AI a Public and Ethical Issue?
proposed subtitle/description: Frame AI as a technical project that redistributes visibility, authority, risk, and responsibility in public life.
proposed rationale for the change: Keep as proposed. Public/ethical framing motivates the course without requiring students to apply evaluative tools they have not yet learned.

module: Module 1. Ethics, Knowledge, Learning, and Intelligence
proposed module classification / title: Module 1. AI-Mediated World: Purposes, Values, Knowledge, and Intelligence
lecture: What Is Knowledge, and How Is It Made?
subtitle: Examine how evidence, interpretation, institutions, and authority shape what comes to count as knowledge.
proposed new topic: What Is AI Supposed to Help Us Do?
revised subtitle: Examine how claims about safety, care, efficiency, creativity, and improvement shape what AI is designed and justified to do.
rationale: This keeps the course anchored in human purposes and values before asking how knowledge is produced or automated.
proposed module title: Module 1. AI-Mediated World: Purposes, Values, and Questions
proposed topic: What Is AI Supposed to Help Us Do?
proposed subtitle/description: Examine how claims about safety, care, efficiency, creativity, and improvement shape what AI is designed and justified to do.
proposed rationale for the change: Keep as proposed. Anchors the course in human purposes before mechanism.

module: Module 1. Ethics, Knowledge, Learning, and Intelligence
proposed module classification / title: Module 1. AI-Mediated World: Purposes, Values, Knowledge, and Intelligence
lecture: What Ethical Lenses Help Us Evaluate AI?
subtitle: Introduce utilitarianism, care ethics, deontology, and the common good as practical ways to assess AI systems.
proposed new topic: What Ethical Lenses Help Us Evaluate AI?
revised subtitle: Use ethical frameworks to move from personal reaction to structured judgment about benefits, harms, duties, care, and the common good.
rationale: Keep this where it is; students need an early vocabulary for taking positions without reducing analysis to personal opinion.
proposed module title: Module 1. AI-Mediated World: Purposes, Values, and Questions
proposed topic: What Tools Will Help Us Take a Position?
proposed subtitle/description: Preview the five course guiding questions as analytical tools. Introduce ethical frameworks as vocabulary to develop all semester rather than conclusions to reach now.
proposed rationale for the change: CHANGE from 'What Ethical Lenses Help Us Evaluate AI?' Students cannot meaningfully apply ethical frameworks before they understand how AI works. Use this session to preview the 5 guiding questions explicitly and introduce ethical frameworks as tools they will develop and apply throughout the course. Move the full applied session to Week 7.

module: Module 1. Ethics, Knowledge, Learning, and Intelligence
proposed module classification / title: Module 1. AI-Mediated World: Purposes, Values, Knowledge, and Intelligence
lecture: What Is Learning, and How Does It Happen?
subtitle: Explore learning as biological, developmental, social, and tool-mediated rather than only individual or computational.
proposed new topic: What Is Knowledge, and How Is It Made?
revised subtitle: Examine how evidence, interpretation, institutions, and authority shape what counts as knowledge before and after AI enters the scene.
rationale: Knowledge fits better before learning: students first ask what counts as knowledge, then how people and systems acquire or produce it.
proposed module title: Module 1. AI-Mediated World: Purposes, Values, and Questions
proposed topic: What Is Knowledge, and How Is It Made?
proposed subtitle/description: Examine how evidence, interpretation, institutions, and authority shape what counts as knowledge before and after AI enters the scene.

module: Module 1. Ethics, Knowledge, Learning, and Intelligence
proposed module classification / title: Module 1. AI-Mediated World: Purposes, Values, Knowledge, and Intelligence
lecture: What Counts as Intelligence?
subtitle: Compare human, animal, machine, social, and embodied intelligence and ask what each definition leaves out.
proposed new topic: What Is Learning, and How Does It Happen?
revised subtitle: Compare biological, developmental, social, tool-mediated, and computational learning to avoid reducing learning to model training.
rationale: Consider pushing intelligence into the transition to Module 2 or pairing it with learning; learning is the bridge to machine learning.
proposed module title: Module 1. AI-Mediated World: Purposes, Values, and Questions
proposed topic: What Is Learning, and How Does It Happen?
proposed subtitle/description: Compare biological, developmental, social, tool-mediated, and computational learning to avoid reducing learning to model training.

proposed module classification / title: Module 2. Technical and Social Mechanisms of Machine Learning
lecture: How Does the World Become Data?
subtitle: Introduce sensing, measurement, categories, baselines, proxies, and features as the first step in machine learning.
proposed new topic: What Counts as Intelligence?
revised subtitle: Compare human, animal, social, embodied, and machine intelligence while asking what each definition emphasizes or leaves out.
rationale: Use this as a hinge: after human/social/biological learning, ask what counts as machine intelligence before datafication begins.
proposed module title: Module 1. AI-Mediated World: Purposes, Values, and Questions
proposed topic: What Counts as Intelligence?
proposed subtitle/description: Compare human, animal, social, embodied, and machine intelligence while asking what each definition emphasizes or leaves out.
proposed rationale for the change: Moved into Module 1. This is a definitional question in the same register as 'What Is Knowledge?' and 'What Is Learning?' — all three ask students to examine a concept they think they already understand. Module 2 then opens cleanly with How Does the World Become Data?

proposed module classification / title: Module 2. Technical and Social Mechanisms of Machine Learning
lecture: What Gets Built Into the Data?
subtitle: Use datasets, proxies, and feature design to ask what assumptions and exclusions are carried into a system.
proposed new topic: How Does the World Become Data?
revised subtitle: Introduce sensing, measurement, categories, baselines, proxies, and features as technical and social acts of translation.
rationale: Shift datafication here so Module 2 opens with the technical/social process of turning the world into inputs.
proposed module title: Module 2. Technical and Social Mechanisms of Machine Learning
proposed topic: How Does the World Become Data?
proposed subtitle/description: Introduce sensing, measurement, categories, baselines, proxies, and features as technical and social acts of translation.

proposed module classification / title: Module 2. Technical and Social Mechanisms of Machine Learning

subtitle: Introduce models, training, inference, and the basic machine learning pipeline.
proposed new topic: What Gets Built Into the Data?
revised subtitle: Use datasets, proxies, and feature design to examine how assumptions, exclusions, histories, and institutional priorities enter AI systems.
rationale: Datasets, labels, proxies, and exclusions should precede the model pipeline so students see training data as already social.
proposed module title: Module 2. Technical and Social Mechanisms of Machine Learning
proposed topic: What Gets Built Into the Data?
proposed subtitle/description: Use datasets, proxies, and feature design to examine how assumptions, exclusions, histories, and institutional priorities enter AI systems.

proposed module classification / title: Module 2. Technical and Social Mechanisms of Machine Learning

revised subtitle: Introduce models, training, inference, and evaluation as the basic pipeline through which data becomes computational judgment.
rationale: Keep the model pipeline before learning paradigms; students need the general pipeline before distinguishing types.
proposed module title: Module 2. Technical and Social Mechanisms of Machine Learning
proposed topic: How Do Machines Learn from Data?
proposed subtitle/description: Introduce models, training, inference, and evaluation as the basic pipeline through which data becomes computational judgment.

proposed module classification / title: Module 2. Technical and Social Mechanisms of Machine Learning

subtitle: Trace how model outputs become scores, rankings, thresholds, and institutional action.

proposed module title: Module 2. Technical and Social Mechanisms of Machine Learning
proposed topic: What Kind of Learning Is This?

proposed module classification / title: Module 2. Technical and Social Mechanisms of Machine Learning

proposed new topic: How Do Scores Become Decisions?
revised subtitle: Trace how model outputs become scores, rankings, thresholds, alerts, recommendations, and institutional actions.
rationale: Scores-to-decisions is the missing bridge between mechanism and institutional action; keep it immediately before error.
proposed module title: Module 2. Technical and Social Mechanisms of Machine Learning
proposed topic: How Do Scores Become Decisions?
proposed subtitle/description: Trace how model outputs become scores, rankings, thresholds, alerts, recommendations, and institutional actions.

proposed module classification / title: Module 2. Technical and Social Mechanisms of Machine Learning
lecture: When Does Prediction Start to Look Like Intelligence?
subtitle: Examine how prediction, pattern recognition, and historical data can make systems seem smart.
proposed new topic: Who Bears the Cost of Error?
revised subtitle: Examine how false positives, false negatives, and uncertainty distribute burdens differently across people and contexts.
rationale: Error should follow thresholds and precede prediction, because prediction only matters socially once errors affect people differently.
proposed module title: Module 2. Technical and Social Mechanisms of Machine Learning
proposed topic: Who Bears the Cost of Error?
proposed subtitle/description: Examine how false positives, false negatives, and uncertainty distribute burdens differently across people and contexts.

proposed module classification / title: Module 2. Technical and Social Mechanisms of Machine Learning
lecture: Are Predictions Ever Neutral?

proposed new topic: When Does Prediction Start to Look Like Intelligence?
revised subtitle: Explore how pattern recognition and historical data can make systems appear smart even when they are extending past regularities.
rationale: This is a strong sequence: prediction-as-intelligence before prediction-as-history/future-making.
proposed module title: Module 2. Technical and Social Mechanisms of Machine Learning
proposed topic: What Ethical Lenses Help Us Evaluate AI?
proposed subtitle/description: Apply utilitarianism, care ethics, deontology, and the common good to concrete AI mechanisms students now understand: error, thresholds, training data, and scores.
proposed rationale for the change: MOVED HERE from Week 2. Students now have enough knowledge of mechanism, error, and thresholds to apply ethical frameworks to real cases. This is when the frameworks grip something concrete. The prediction-as-intelligence topic folds into Week 8 Thu.
